chunk_summary: Standardized anthropometry and targeted physical exams are essential for metabolic risk stratification in children, focusing on waist-to-height ratio, Tanner staging, and specific physical findings to guide downstream evaluations.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of standardized anthropometric measurements in pediatric metabolic risk assessment.
Learn to identify physical exam findings that trigger specific diagnostic pathways, such as acanthosis nigricans or hepatomegaly.
Recognize the role of pubertal staging in interpreting metabolic risk markers during adolescence.
Apply screening heuristics like waist-to-height ratio >0.5 in the absence of waist percentiles.
key_facts: Waist-to-height ratio >0.5 can be used as a screening heuristic for metabolic risk in children.
Pubertal insulin resistance during mid-puberty may transiently worsen triglycerides and glycemia, requiring earlier follow-up for borderline values.
Acanthosis nigricans indicates insulin resistance and should prompt dysglycemia evaluation, but not routine fasting insulin or HOMA-IR testing.
Hepatomegaly or right upper quadrant tenderness should trigger MASLD exclusion work-up rather than reliance on ultrasound alone.
OSA evaluation is recommended for children with snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, or poor school performance.
reference_quote: Anthropometry should be standardized so trends drive decisions rather than noise. Measure waist in a standing child at the end of normal expiration using a stretch-resistant tape kept horizontal and snug without compressing skin. — IAP guidelines on childhood obesity, 2023
document_key_topics: Physical exam findings and diagnostic pathways
Screening for OSA and vascular complications
